title: Scaling & Serving
eli8:
# 📈 Handling Millions of Users!

Imagine you're running a hot dog stand. One customer? Easy! But what happens when 1000 people show up at once? 🌭

You need smart tricks to handle everyone without making them wait forever.

## 🚌 Batching: The Bus Trick

Instead of one hot dog (request) at a time, you cook 20 hot dogs together on the grill!

- **Without batching**: AI processes 1 question, then another, then another
- **With batching**: AI processes 20 questions at the same time on the GPU
- It's like a bus vs. 20 individual taxis — same destination, much more efficient! 🚌

**vLLM** is a special server that does this automatically — it's the magic bus driver for AI!

## 🔮 Speculative Decoding: Guessing Ahead

Imagine you're typing a text message and your phone guesses the next word. If it guesses right, you just tap it!

Speculative decoding works the same way:
1. A tiny, fast model **guesses** the next few words
2. The big model **verifies** the guess in one step
3. If correct, you skip ahead — 2-3x faster! ⚡
4. If wrong, the big model corrects it

## 📋 Remember Common Starting Points (Prefix Caching)

If every user starts with the same long instructions (like "You are a helpful assistant for..."), why process them again and again?

**Prefix caching** saves the processed version of common beginnings and reuses it instantly. It's like keeping a pre-made pizza base in the fridge — you start from there instead of making dough every time! 🍕

## 🔄 Auto-Scaling: Automatic Hiring

When your restaurant gets super busy, you call in extra staff. When it's quiet, they go home.

**Auto-scaling** adds more server copies when traffic is high and removes them when traffic is low — you only pay for what you're actually using! Perfect for AI apps with busy periods and quiet times. 🌊
detail:
## Scaling & Serving LLM Applications

As LLM applications grow beyond prototype stage, the serving infrastructure becomes critical. Naive serving (one request at a time) wastes GPU memory and throughput. Modern serving systems use several techniques to maximize efficiency.

### Dynamic Batching with vLLM

**vLLM** is an open-source LLM serving engine that implements **continuous batching** (also called iteration-level scheduling):

- Traditional batching: wait for N requests, process together, wait, process
- Continuous batching: as soon as one sequence finishes, add a new request into its GPU slot
- Result: GPU utilization goes from ~30% (naive) to >90%
- Throughput improvement: 10-24× over naive serving (vLLM paper benchmark)

```bash
# Launch vLLM server
pip install vllm
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
  --model meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf \
  --max-model-len 4096

# It exposes an OpenAI-compatible API automatically
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions ...
```

### Speculative Decoding

LLMs generate tokens auto-regressively — one at a time. Speculative decoding breaks this bottleneck:

1. A small **draft model** (3-7B params) quickly generates k candidate tokens
2. The large **target model** verifies all k tokens in a single forward pass
3. If draft tokens are accepted, you've produced k tokens in 1 large-model pass
4. Any rejected token triggers fallback to the target model's generation

**Result**: 2-3× speedup on text with predictable patterns (code, structured text). Works best when draft and target models share the same tokenizer.

```python
# Hugging Face supports speculative decoding natively
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

target = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1")
draft = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B")

outputs = target.generate(
    inputs, assistant_model=draft, do_sample=False
)
```

### Prefix Caching (KV Cache Reuse)

The Attention mechanism computes Key-Value tensors for every token. When many requests share a common prefix (e.g., a system prompt), those KV computations can be cached and reused:

- vLLM supports automatic prefix caching with `--enable-prefix-caching`
- 40-70% latency reduction on requests with repeated system prompts
- Especially powerful for RAG (same retrieved docs used for multiple turns)

### Auto-Scaling with Kubernetes

**Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)** scales replicas based on metrics:

```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: llm-api-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    kind: Deployment
    name: llm-api
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: External
    external:
      metric:
        name: queue_depth
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: 5
```

For GPU workloads: scale on GPU utilization or queue depth, not CPU.

### Load Balancing Strategies

- **Round-robin**: requests distributed evenly. Simple, works for homogeneous requests
- **Least-connections**: route to instance with fewest active requests. Better for variable-length LLM responses
- **Latency-aware**: route to fastest-responding instance. Best for user-facing services

### Multi-GPU Serving

**Tensor parallelism**: split a single model's weight matrices across multiple GPUs. Each GPU holds a slice and communicates via NVLink. Enables serving models too large for one GPU.

```bash
# vLLM with 4 GPUs via tensor parallelism
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
  --model meta-llama/Llama-2-70b-chat-hf \
  --tensor-parallel-size 4
```

**Pipeline parallelism**: split layers across GPUs (layer 0-15 on GPU0, layer 16-31 on GPU1). Lower communication overhead than tensor parallelism but introduces pipeline latency.

**DeepSpeed-Inference** extends these with ZeRO-Inference memory optimization for serving models with limited GPU memory.

### Scaling RAG Systems

- Multiple vector DB replicas for read parallelism
- Read replicas for ChromaDB/pgvector; Pinecone/Weaviate scale horizontally
- Embedding generation is stateless — easy to run as independent auto-scaling service
- Cache embeddings for frequently retrieved documents (CDN or Redis)

### Capacity Planning Rule of Thumb

- Benchmark single-instance throughput under realistic load
- Target 60-70% utilization (headroom for spikes)
- Plan for 2× expected peak (product launches, viral moments)
- Keep scale-up time < 2 minutes for user-facing services

mcqs.question: What is the key improvement of continuous batching (vLLM) over traditional static batching?
options: It compresses model weights to fit on a single GPU
As soon as one sequence finishes, a new request immediately fills that GPU slot — maintaining near-constant throughput without waiting for the full batch to finish
It uses multiple small models instead of one large model
It defers batch processing to off-peak hours to reduce cost
mcqs.explanation: In static batching, the entire batch must complete before new requests join. Continuous batching adds new requests to free slots as sequences complete, dramatically increasing GPU utilization and overall throughput.

mcqs.question: In speculative decoding, what is the role of the 'draft model'?
options: It is a cached version of the target model from a previous request
A small, fast model that generates candidate token sequences which the larger target model then verifies in a single pass
A model trained specifically on the task domain for higher accuracy
A quantized version of the target model used as a fallback when the target is busy
mcqs.explanation: The draft model (small, fast) quickly produces k candidate tokens. The target model verifies all k candidates simultaneously in one forward pass. Accepted tokens represent a speedup; rejected tokens fall back to target-model generation.

mcqs.question: What does prefix caching optimize in LLM serving?
options: It compresses system prompts using gzip before sending to the model
It reuses pre-computed Key-Value (KV) cache tensors for common prompt prefixes, avoiding redundant attention computation
It routes requests with matching prefixes to the same GPU for better cache locality
It stores the first 100 tokens of every response for auto-completion
mcqs.explanation: The attention mechanism computes KV tensors for every token. When requests share a prefix (like a system prompt), those KV computations can be stored and reused, reducing latency by 40-70% for matching requests.

mcqs.question: What is tensor parallelism in multi-GPU LLM serving?
options: Running multiple independent copies of the model on separate GPUs for throughput
Splitting a model's weight matrices across multiple GPUs so each holds a fraction, enabling serving models too large for a single GPU
Distributing different layers of the model across GPUs sequentially
A method of compressing model weights using tensor decomposition
mcqs.explanation: Tensor parallelism splits individual weight matrices across GPUs — each GPU holds a slice and they collaborate via NVLink. This allows a 70B model that requires 140GB VRAM to run across 4×40GB A100s.

mcqs.question: For a Kubernetes HPA scaling an LLM API, what metric is generally better than CPU utilization for triggering scale-up?
options: Disk I/O throughput
Network egress bytes per second
Request queue depth or GPU utilization — because LLMs are GPU-bound, not CPU-bound
Memory swap usage
mcqs.explanation: LLM inference workloads are GPU-bound. CPU utilization stays low while GPUs are saturated. Scaling based on GPU utilization or request queue depth triggers scale-up when the actual bottleneck (the GPU) is saturated.

mcqs.question: Pipeline parallelism differs from tensor parallelism in that:
options: Pipeline parallelism works only for training, not inference
Pipeline parallelism splits model layers across GPUs sequentially; tensor parallelism splits individual matrices within each layer
Pipeline parallelism requires the same model architecture on each GPU
Pipeline parallelism is strictly faster than tensor parallelism in all cases
mcqs.explanation: In pipeline parallelism, layers 0-N run on GPU0, layers N+1-2N on GPU1, etc. In tensor parallelism, each layer's matrices are split across all GPUs simultaneously. Tensor parallelism has higher communication overhead but lower latency; pipeline has lower communication but introduces pipeline bubbles.

flashcards.back: An LLM serving technique (implemented in vLLM) where free GPU slots are filled immediately as sequences complete, rather than waiting for a full static batch to finish. Achieves 10-24× throughput improvement over naive serving.

A serving optimization where a small draft model generates k candidate tokens which the target model verifies in one parallel pass. Accepted tokens yield a 2-3× speedup. Best for text with predictable patterns.
Storing pre-computed KV-cache tensors for common prompt prefixes (e.g., system prompts). Subsequent requests reusing that prefix skip recomputing those tokens, reducing latency 40-70%.
A multi-GPU technique that splits individual weight matrices across GPUs, each holding a fraction. GPUs communicate via NVLink. Enables serving models too large for a single GPU's memory.

A multi-GPU technique where model layers are split sequentially across GPUs (GPU0 runs layers 0-15, GPU1 runs layers 16-31). Lower communication overhead than tensor parallelism but introduces inter-stage latency.
A Kubernetes controller that automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed metrics (CPU, GPU utilization, queue depth). Scales up during peak load and down during quiet periods.
Another term for dynamic batching — the key property is that the batch composition changes at every token generation step, not just between full requests. New requests join as slots free up.

videos.title: vLLM: High-Throughput LLM Serving
videos.description: Technical deep dive into vLLM's continuous batching, PagedAttention, and prefix caching mechanisms that enable high-throughput LLM serving on commodity GPUs.

videos.title: LLM Serving at Scale: From 1 User to 1 Million
videos.description: Practical walkthrough of scaling LLM serving infrastructure — dynamic batching, speculative decoding, multi-GPU tensor parallelism, and Kubernetes auto-scaling patterns.
